title: Intersections and the Bezout Range
speaker: David Urbanik
abstract:
Let A be a simple abelian variety, and X and Y be two subvarieties. We say X and Y are in the Bezout range if \dim X + \dim Y greater than or equal to \dim A, and outside of the Bezout range otherwise. It is known that two varieties in the Bezout range in A always intersect. In this talk we explain that, after multiplying Y by some endomorphism n, we can make them intersect properly, and moreover such intersections give an analytically dense set of intersections with X. Moreover in the case where X and Y are outside the Bezout range, we show that X and n Y almost never intersect, except in the presence of torsion points.
Joint work with Greg Baldi.

proof_stages.label: Bezout-range and classical translation benchmarks
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up proper intersection, defines the Bezout range, recalls Bezout-type nonemptiness in projective space, and explains how Barth’s theorem for simple abelian varieties plus Kleiman transversality solves the analogous problem for translations.

proof_stages.label: Endomorphism-intersection problem and main results
proof_stages.summary: The central question is to replace translating Y by multiplying it by n, and the main theorems assert eventual properness and analytic density inside the range, and a density-one torsion-only statement outside the range.

proof_stages.label: Unlikely-intersection reformulation by graphs
proof_stages.summary: Properness of X∩[n]Y is reformulated as properness of (Y×X)∩Γ_n in A×A, after which Γ_n is viewed among special subvarieties and the relevant Zilber-Pink philosophy is recalled.

proof_stages.label: Inside-range finiteness via geometric Zilber-Pink
proof_stages.summary: Inside the Bezout range, nonproper intersections with Γ_n must have positive-dimensional atypical components, so Habegger’s finite-family theorem for weakly special intersections implies that only finitely many n can be bad.

proof_stages.label: Outside-range reduction to definable non-torsion parameters
proof_stages.summary: Outside the range the torsion points are separated off by Manin-Mumford/Raynaud, while non-torsion atypical points are lifted through the uniformization of A and encoded by a definable set S of rational parameters for lifted linear spaces L_{n,a}.

proof_stages.label: Pila-Wilkie counting and algebraic-curve obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Pila-Wilkie implies that too many rational parameters in S would force a real algebraic curve of parameters, producing an algebraic family of lifted linear spaces whose intersection with the lifted variety is unexpectedly one-dimensional.

proof_stages.label: Ax-theoretic contradiction and density-one conclusion
proof_stages.summary: Ax’s theorem places the projected unexpected curve in one of the preselected strict weakly special translates, and the defining compatibility of S then forces a bi-algebraic degeneracy, yielding subpolynomially many bad parameters and hence the density-one outside-range result.

proof_stages.label: Analytic density of inside-range intersections
proof_stages.summary: The analytic-density statement is proved heuristically by combining equidistribution of multiples of points of Y, flattening of [n]Y at large n, and openness of transverse intersections for nearby analytic germs.

examples.name: Non-simple product abelian variety obstruction
examples.note: A product/non-simple abelian variety can contain large disjoint translates still lying in the Bezout range, showing why the simplicity hypothesis is needed rather than merely decorative.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the product case A=A1+A2 with subvarieties supported in different factors, checks they do not intersect for suitable distinct points, and notes dimensions can still put them in the Bezout range.

passes.reason: A decomposable product abelian variety is a standard non-simple member, used to show Barth-style nonempty intersection fails without simplicity.
A decomposable product abelian variety with disjoint large cosets is an ordinary non-simple member, used to show the Barth nonemptiness statement fails without simplicity.
A decomposable product abelian variety with coordinate-type subvarieties is used to show Barth-style nonemptiness can fail without simplicity.
A decomposable product abelian variety is not structurally exceptional here, but it is used to show Barth's conclusion fails without simplicity.

a decomposable product abelian variety is used to show Barth-style nonempty intersection can fail without simplicity, while its product structure is not exceptional/degenerate/extremal/symmetric in the strict sense
A decomposable product abelian variety with disjoint translates of a large factor is an ordinary non-simple member, used to show the simplicity hypothesis is necessary.
A decomposable product abelian variety with separated factor-translates is used to show Barth's nonemptiness statement fails without simplicity.
A decomposable product abelian variety with fiber subvarieties in the Bezout range but disjoint is used to show the simplicity hypothesis cannot be dropped.
A decomposable product abelian variety with disjoint factor-translates in the Bezout range is used to show Barth's conclusion fails without simplicity.
It is used to show Barth-type nonemptiness fails without the simplicity assumption, while a decomposable product abelian variety is part of a systematic infinite class.

A decomposable product abelian variety with coordinate-type subvarieties is used to show Barth-style nonemptiness fails without the simplicity hypothesis, but it is a systematic non-simple case rather than an extremal, exceptional, symmetric, or degenerate object.
A decomposable product abelian variety is used to show Barth-type nonempty intersection fails without simplicity, but it is not sporadic, limiting, extremal, or maximally regular.

examples.name: Torsion obstructions outside the range
examples.note: A common torsion point such as 0 in both X and Y remains in X∩[n]Y for every n even when the expected intersection is empty, and higher torsion congruences explain the torsion exception and natural-density formulation.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces torsion points such as 0 through multiplication by n, showing they remain in X∩nY and hence obstruct proper intersection outside the range.

passes.reason: Finite-order zero-dimensional torsion points are minimal/trivial special cases, used as unavoidable obstructions to empty proper intersections outside the range.
A shared torsion point, especially the zero point, is a collapsed finite-order special point that obstructs the outside-range expectation of empty/proper intersections.
Zero or torsion points give finite-order trivial intersections fixed under suitable multiplication maps, obstructing the expected empty intersection outside the Bezout range.
The zero/torsion-point obstruction is a minimal zero-dimensional special case, used to show outside-range intersections need not be empty.
torsion points in both subvarieties obstruct the expected empty proper intersection outside the Bezout range, but finite-order points are not structurally sporadic or extremal here
The persistent zero or torsion point is a minimal zero-dimensional obstruction to the expected empty intersection outside the Bezout range.
Torsion, especially the zero point lying in both subvarieties, is used as a finite-order obstruction to the expected empty intersection outside the Bezout range.
A common torsion point, especially the zero point, persists in X∩[n]Y outside the range and obstructs the expected empty/proper intersection; the point is a trivial zero-dimensional special case.
A common torsion point, especially the zero point, persists under multiplication and obstructs the expected empty proper intersection outside the range.
Common torsion points such as 0 obstruct the expected empty intersection outside the Bezout range, and such zero-dimensional finite-order points are minimal/trivial cases.

Common torsion points such as 0 persist in x∩[n]y outside the Bezout range and obstruct the expected empty proper intersection, with the point obstruction being a zero-dimensional/trivial special case.

A torsion, especially zero, point lying in both varieties persists under multiplication and obstructs the expected empty intersection outside the range, and as a zero-dimensional translate of the trivial subgroup it is degenerate.

examples.name: Shimura-variety and Hecke-operator analogue
examples.note: In Q&A, Shimura varieties with Hecke correspondences are mentioned as a parallel setting where the proof’s uniqueness and no-subvariety features can fail, clarifying which abelian-variety properties the argument uses.
examples.engagement_reason: The Shimura/Hecke analogue is discussed only as a prospective extension with stated subtleties about Hecke operators and special subvarieties, without working through a concrete Shimura example.

passes.reason: Shimura varieties with Hecke correspondences form a systematic highly symmetric analogue rather than a sporadic or extremal object, discussed as a possible extension rather than a disproof.
Shimura varieties with Hecke correspondences are locally symmetric arithmetic objects, raised as a possible analogue/generalization rather than as a refutation.
Shimura varieties with Hecke correspondences are discussed as a locally symmetric analogue and possible extension rather than as a specific failure example.
Shimura varieties with Hecke correspondences are locally symmetric/arithmetic analogues, invoked as a possible generalization rather than as a failure case.
Shimura varieties with Hecke operators are a highly symmetric locally symmetric analogue being discussed as an extension rather than deployed as a disproof
Shimura varieties with Hecke correspondences form a locally symmetric analogue raised as a possible extension rather than as a refutation.
The Shimura/Hecke setting is invoked as a prospective analogue rather than a refutation, and Shimura varieties are locally symmetric objects with Hecke symmetries.
Shimura varieties with Hecke correspondences are raised as a prospective symmetric-domain analogue rather than as a refutation of a claim.
Shimura varieties with Hecke correspondences are invoked as a prospective symmetric-space analogue rather than as a refutation.
It is raised as a possible analogue/generalization rather than a refutation, and Shimura varieties are built from Hermitian symmetric domains with Hecke symmetries.

Shimura varieties with Hecke operators are raised as an analogous setting rather than as a concrete refutation, and their structure is intrinsically symmetric via arithmetic locally symmetric geometry.
The Shimura-Hecke setting is raised as a prospective analogue rather than a refutation, and Shimura varieties are intrinsically locally symmetric arithmetic varieties.
